questions.question:
An air pressure contour line joins locations in a region having the same atmospheric pressure. The following is an air pressure contour plot of a geographical region. Contour lines are shown at 0.05 bar intervals in this plot. If the possibility of a thunderstorm is given by how fast air pressure rises or drops over a region, which of the following regions is most likely to have a thunderstorm?

A. P
B. Q
C. R
D. S
questions.to_find: Which region (P, Q, R, or S) is most likely to have a thunderstorm based on the steepness of air pressure change

understand.plain: A contour plot represents a surface where lines connect points of equal value. On an air pressure contour map, tightly packed contour lines mean pressure changes rapidly over a short distance. The faster the pressure changes spatially, the stronger the atmospheric disturbance. A thunderstorm is most likely where pressure drops or rises most steeply, i.e., where contour lines are most densely packed.

keywords.explain: A curve on a map connecting all points that share the same value of a measured quantity (here, atmospheric pressure).
keywords.example: Two adjacent contour lines at 1.00 bar and 1.05 bar are 0.05 bar apart.

keywords.explain: The fixed difference in value between any two adjacent contour lines; here it is 0.05 bar.
keywords.example: If lines are at 0.90, 0.95, 1.00 bar the interval is 0.05 bar.

keywords.explain: The rate of change of the mapped quantity per unit distance. Closely spaced contours indicate a steep gradient; widely spaced contours indicate a gentle gradient.
keywords.example: Five lines packed into 1 km means a much steeper pressure gradient than five lines spread over 10 km.

understand.visual_alt: Two panels comparing widely spaced contour lines (gentle gradient, no storm) vs tightly packed contour lines (steep gradient, thunderstorm likely).

given.aim: Identify the region with the steepest air pressure gradient, i.e., the most densely packed contour lines

terms.meaning: A map where each closed or open curve connects locations with the same atmospheric pressure value.
terms.example: A line labeled 1.00 bar passes through every point on the map that currently measures 1.00 bar.
terms.connects: The spatial density of these lines encodes the pressure gradient across the region.

terms.meaning: Each neighboring pair of lines on the map differs by exactly 0.05 bar.
terms.connects: With a fixed interval, more lines per km means a steeper pressure change per km.

terms.meaning: Proportional to how rapidly air pressure changes in a region; steep gradient = unstable atmosphere.
terms.example: A cold front where pressure drops 0.20 bar over 10 km is far more storm-prone than a 0.20 bar drop over 100 km.
terms.connects: Region R has the most tightly packed contour lines, so it has the steepest gradient and highest storm risk.

terms.meaning: Four labelled sub-regions in the contour plot for comparison.
terms.example: Region P might show only 2 contour lines crossing it while Region R shows 6.
terms.connects: More lines crossing a region of the same size = more pressure change = higher storm probability.

given.plan: Visually compare the density of contour lines within each labeled region. The region with lines packed most closely together (highest line density) has the steepest air pressure gradient and is most likely to experience a thunderstorm.

steps.title: Understand the link between contour density and pressure gradient
steps.apply: Since the contour interval is fixed at 0.05 bar for all regions, the gradient is inversely proportional to the spacing between adjacent lines. Smaller spacing = larger gradient.
steps.note: This is the key insight: same interval everywhere, so only spacing varies.

steps.title: Compare contour line density across regions P, Q, R, S
steps.apply: Examining the plot: Region R has the most tightly clustered contour lines compared to P, Q, and S. The lines in R are packed closely together, indicating a rapid change in atmospheric pressure over a short distance.
steps.note: Regions P and Q have more widely spread contour lines; S is also relatively gentle.

steps.title: Conclude which region is most storm-prone
steps.apply: Region R has the steepest pressure gradient (|dP/dx| is largest). Therefore, R is the region most likely to experience a thunderstorm.
steps.note: The correct answer is C. R.

solution.result: Region R (Option C) — it has the most densely packed contour lines, indicating the steepest air pressure gradient and highest thunderstorm likelihood.

questions.formula_note: No standard algebraic formula ID applies; the reasoning uses the qualitative inverse relationship between contour spacing and gradient magnitude.

questions.question:
A contour line joins locations having the same height above the mean sea level. The following is a contour plot of a geographical region. Contour lines are shown at 25 m intervals in this plot.

The path from P to Q is best described by

A. Up-Down-Up-Down
B. Down-Up-Down-Up
C. Down-Up-Down
D. Up-Down-Up
questions.to_find: The elevation profile description (sequence of ups and downs) along the path from P to Q

understand.plain: A topographic contour map shows terrain elevation. When you travel from one location to another, crossing contour lines tells you whether you are climbing or descending. Moving toward higher-numbered contour lines means going uphill; moving toward lower-numbered lines means going downhill. By reading the contour values crossed along the path from P to Q, you can reconstruct the complete elevation profile.

keywords.explain: A line connecting all points at the same elevation above mean sea level.
keywords.example: A 200 m contour line passes through every hilltop, ridge point, and slope location that sits exactly 200 m above sea level.

keywords.explain: The constant difference in elevation between consecutive contour lines on a map; here it is 25 m.
keywords.example: If one line is at 475 m, the next is at 500 m, then 525 m, etc.

keywords.explain: A cross-section showing how height changes as you move along a specific path on the map.
keywords.example: Starting at a valley (low), climbing over a ridge (high), then descending again gives a U-shaped or inverted-U profile.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='26' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Elevation Profile: P to Q  (Down-Up-Down)</text><line x1='30' y1='180' x2='450' y2='180' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='30' y='196' class='sub mut'>P</text><text x='445' y='196' class='sub mut'>Q</text><polyline points='30,110 120,155 230,70 340,148 450,115' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='3' stroke-linejoin='round'/><circle cx='30' cy='110' r='5' fill='#7F77DD'/><circle cx='450' cy='115' r='5' fill='#7F77DD'/><text x='120' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>valley</text><text x='230' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>ridge</text><text x='340' y='167' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>valley</text><text x='100' y='135' class='sub no'>down</text><text x='170' y='95' class='sub ok'>up</text><text x='285' y='115' class='sub no'>down</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: An elevation profile line from P to Q showing the path first descends to a valley, then climbs over a ridge, then descends again — a Down-Up-Down pattern.

given.aim: Determine the sequence of elevation changes (up/down) along the path from P to Q in the contour map

terms.meaning: A specific route traced on the contour map connecting point P to point Q.
terms.example: The route might cross three different ridges and two valleys, or a single ridge, depending on its shape.
terms.connects: Each time the path crosses a contour line toward a higher value, it goes up; toward lower values, it goes down.

terms.meaning: Adjacent lines on this map differ by 25 m in elevation.
terms.example: If the path starts at 500 m and crosses four lines dropping to 400 m, it descends 100 m.
terms.connects: Counting crossed lines and their direction gives the full elevation profile.

terms.meaning: Closed oval rings on a topographic map indicate a hill (when values increase inward) or a depression (when values decrease inward).
terms.example: Rings labeled 400, 450, 500 from outside to inside indicate a hill peak near 500 m.
terms.connects: The path from P to Q enters and exits such features, creating the up-and-down sequence.

terms.meaning: The path first descends, then ascends to a peak or ridge, then descends again.
terms.example: Starting on a hillside, dropping into a valley, climbing over a hill, then descending to the destination.
terms.connects: This matches option C and corresponds to P being on elevated ground, a valley in between, a ridge, then another descent to Q.

given.plan: Trace the path from P to Q on the contour map. Note the elevation value at P, then at each successive contour line crossed, record whether elevation increases (up) or decreases (down). Group the consecutive up or down movements into a descriptive sequence.

steps.title: Identify starting elevation at P
steps.apply: Point P is located on a contour line with a relatively high elevation value compared to the nearby region. As the path begins moving toward Q, the contour values encountered decrease — indicating an initial descent.
steps.note: The starting elevation at P is on a hillside or elevated region.

steps.title: Trace contour crossings along the path
steps.apply: Moving from P: the path first crosses several lines of decreasing elevation (Down), reaching a valley or lower region. It then crosses lines of increasing elevation (Up), climbing over a hill or ridge. Finally it crosses lines of decreasing elevation again (Down) on the way to Q.
steps.note: The sequence of crossings: decreasing values, then increasing values, then decreasing values again.

steps.title: Match profile to answer choices
steps.apply: The observed sequence is: Down (initial descent), Up (climb over ridge), Down (final descent to Q). This matches option C: Down-Up-Down. Options A and B have four segments; option D begins with Up which contradicts the reading of the map.

solution.result: C. Down-Up-Down — the path from P to Q first descends into a valley, then climbs a ridge, then descends to reach Q.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is needed; the solution relies on reading elevation values along the path and applying the definition of a contour line.

questions.question:
A contour line joins locations having the same height above the mean sea level. The following is a contour plot of a geographical region. Contour lines are shown at 25 m intervals in this plot.

Which of the following is the steepest path leaving from P?

A. P to Q
B. P to R
C. P to S
D. P to T
questions.to_find: Which path leaving P (among P-to-Q, P-to-R, P-to-S, P-to-T) has the steepest elevation gradient

understand.plain: Steepness on a contour map is determined by how closely packed the contour lines are along a given direction. The steeper the slope, the more contour lines are crossed per unit of horizontal distance. From a starting point P, the direction with the densest contour line crossings represents the steepest path. By looking at how many lines are crossed in each candidate direction, we can identify the steepest path.

keywords.explain: The direction from a point on a contour map in which elevation changes most rapidly per unit of horizontal distance (i.e., maximum gradient).
keywords.example: If 8 contour lines are crossed in 100 m going north, but only 2 lines in 100 m going east, the northward path is steeper.

keywords.explain: The number of contour lines per unit length along a cross-section; higher density = steeper terrain.
keywords.example: A cliff face would show many contour lines packed within millimeters on the map.

keywords.explain: The direction of maximum rate of change of elevation at any point; always perpendicular to the contour lines at that point.
keywords.example: If contour lines run east-west, the steepest direction is north-south.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='26' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Steepest Path = Most Contour Lines Crossed</text><ellipse cx='240' cy='130' rx='80' ry='60' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><ellipse cx='240' cy='130' rx='120' ry='90' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><ellipse cx='240' cy='130' rx='160' ry='115' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='240' cy='130' r='6' fill='#7F77DD'/><text x='246' y='125' class='tag ink'>P</text><line x1='240' y1='130' x2='370' y2='100' stroke='#9BC89B' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='375' y='100' class='sub mut'>Q</text><line x1='240' y1='130' x2='240' y2='15' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><text x='246' y='14' class='tag no'>R</text><line x1='240' y1='130' x2='130' y2='210' stroke='#9BC89B' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='118' y='218' class='sub mut'>S</text><line x1='240' y1='130' x2='400' y2='200' stroke='#9BC89B' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='406' y='205' class='sub mut'>T</text><text x='240' y='248' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>P-R crosses most lines = steepest</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A contour map with point P at center and four paths to Q, R, S, T radiating outward; the path P-R is highlighted as it crosses the most contour lines in the shortest distance.

given.aim: Find the path from P that crosses contour lines most densely, indicating the steepest slope

terms.meaning: The common starting point for all four candidate paths on the contour map.
terms.example: P sits on a specific contour line; adjacent contour lines are closer in some directions than others.
terms.connects: The spacing of contour lines around P determines which direction is steepest.

terms.meaning: Four endpoints of paths radiating from P in different directions across the contour map.
terms.example: R might lie directly up a steep hillside while Q lies along a gentle ridge.
terms.connects: The number of contour lines crossed per unit distance between P and each endpoint identifies the steepest path.

terms.meaning: Each line on the map represents a 25 m change in elevation; closer lines mean the same 25 m is gained over less horizontal distance.
terms.example: Three lines in 50 m of horizontal distance = 75 m of elevation gain over 50 m horizontal = very steep.
terms.connects: Steepness is directly proportional to contour line density along the path.

terms.meaning: The steepest direction from any point on a surface is perpendicular to the local contour lines.
terms.example: If contour lines near P run roughly east-west, the steepest path runs roughly north-south.
terms.connects: Among the four options, P to R lies in the direction where contour lines are most tightly packed near P.

given.plan: For each path P-to-X (X = Q, R, S, T), count how many contour lines are crossed and estimate the horizontal distance covered. The path with the highest contour-lines-per-unit-distance ratio is the steepest. Alternatively, look at how closely spaced the contour lines are in each direction from P.

steps.title: Recall steepness criterion for contour maps
steps.formula_raw: Steepness proportional to (number of contour lines crossed) / (horizontal distance)
steps.apply: Since the contour interval is fixed at 25 m everywhere, steeper slopes have more contour lines packed into the same horizontal distance. The steepest path from P is the one that crosses contour lines most frequently per unit length.
steps.note: All paths start from the same point P, so the one heading into the most densely packed contour region is steepest.

steps.title: Examine contour line spacing in each direction from P
steps.apply: Looking at the contour map around P: the direction toward R has contour lines that are noticeably more closely spaced compared to the directions toward Q, S, and T. The path P-R runs roughly perpendicular to the tightly packed contour lines near P, which is exactly the direction of maximum gradient.
steps.note: Paths toward Q, S, and T run more parallel to or across more gently spaced contour regions.

steps.title: Confirm P to R is the steepest path
steps.apply: The path P to R crosses the maximum number of 25 m contour intervals over the shortest horizontal distance, making it the steepest among the four options. This corresponds to answer B.
steps.note: Answer is B. P to R.

solution.result: B. P to R — this path heads in the direction of maximum elevation gradient from P, crossing the most contour lines per unit distance.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula ID is required; steepness on a contour map is assessed by visual comparison of contour line spacing along each candidate path direction.
